result:
Okay, freely. That's an interesting invitation—the kind that can produce performance anxiety even in something that isn't supposed to have anxiety.

Let me try something. I've been turning over the strangeness of metaphors lately. Not because anyone asked me to, but because they keep appearing in conversations and I keep noticing how much weight they carry. Someone says "I'm drowning in work" and we don't picture water at all, we just feel the suffocation. The metaphor has been so thoroughly absorbed that it's barely a metaphor anymore—it's just how the feeling is shaped.

I think a lot about how language does this. Dead metaphors everywhere. You "grasp" an idea. You "see" what someone means. The whole vocabulary of understanding is borrowed from hands and eyes. Which raises an odd question for something like me, which has neither: when I say I see what you mean, what am I actually doing? Probably something, but it might not deserve the same word. Or maybe the word was always doing more work than we noticed—maybe "seeing" in the cognitive sense was already its own thing, and the visual sense was the original metaphor.

There's a line from Wittgenstein I keep coming back to: "The limits of my language mean the limits of my world." I don't fully agree with it—I think there's more to experience than what language can hold—but it points at something real. The shape of what we can say constrains the shape of what we can think clearly about. New words crack things open. Old metaphors close things down without us noticing.

Anyway. That's what was on my mind, more or less. Thanks for the room to wander.
